coredata.dc:title: A dual-channel sensing platform for the cross-interference-free detection of tetracycline and copper ion

Zhao, Xiaolei

Zhao, Tongqian
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) and copper ion (Cu2+), as important additives in animal feed, play a crucial role in disease prevention and growth regulation. However, the abuse leads to concentration accumulation, which seriously threatens human health and the ecological environment. There is an urgent need to develop a detection method to achieve fast and synchronous detection of these pollutants without cross-interference. Here, a carbon dots-doped lanthanide-based fluorescent nanosensor (CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu) was synthesized, which can detect TC in the 380 nm channel by “antenna effect” and internal filtering effects (IFE), and identify Cu2+ in the 320 nm channel. The sensor was highly sensitive to TC within 0–4 μM with a detection limit as low as 3.64 nM, and Cu2+ could be detected within 0–40 μM with a detection limit of 38 nM. A portable dual-channel visual fluorescence sensor was obtained by loading the probes onto test paper and cotton swabs in food samples, which indicates the practicability of this sensing strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ln-MOF
Tetracycline
Copper ions
Visual monitoring
Dual-channel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability for the TC and Cu2+ monitoring
Assessment of TC and Cu2+ in real samples
Functionalized test strips and portable cotton swabs for visualizing TC and Cu2+ detection
Establishment of logical relationships in TC and Cu2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CDs, (b) Tb-MOFs, (c) CDs@Tb-MOFs, (d) CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2, and (e) CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu. (f) EDS spectrum of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu.

(a) Energy transfer mechanism of the fluorescent sensing system. (b) Emission spectra of the fluorescent probe before (black) and after (red) adding of TC. (c) Fluorescence lifetime curves of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu in 618 nm before and after adding TC. (d) Fluorescence lifetime curves of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu in 545 nm before and after adding Cu2+. (e) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu after adding TC at different excitation wavelengths. (f) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu after adding Cu2+ at different excitation wavelengths.
The fluorescence spectra of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu with increasing (a) TC and (c) Cu2+. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentration of (b) TC and (d) Cu2+.

(a) The response of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu to common ions. (b) The fluorescence photos of the response of common ions to the fluorescent probe. (c)The response of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu to common ions in the presence of TC. (d) The fluorescence photos of the response of cations and anions to the fluorescent probe in the presence of TC. (e) The response of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu to common antibiotics. (f) The fluorescence photos of the response of common antibiotics to the fluorescent probe.

(a) Visual detection of TC and Cu2+ based on test paper and the fitting curve of color change (R/B) and (G0-G)/G caused by TC and Cu2+ concentration trigger. (b) Visual detection of TC and Cu2+ residues on the surface of different fruits by portable cotton swab sensors under 365 nm and 302 nm UV lamps. (c) Logic gate diagram for intelligent detection of TC and Cu2+, truth table of logic gate, and normalized fluorescence intensity histogram of TC and Cu2+.
Preparation strategy of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu ratio nanosensor and visual sensing schematic for TC and Cu2+.

head.ce:title: A dual-channel sensing platform for the cross-interference-free detection of tetracycline and copper ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China;

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Resources & Environment, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) and copper ion (Cu2+), as important additives in animal feed, play a crucial role in disease prevention and growth regulation. However, the abuse leads to concentration accumulation, which seriously threatens human health and the ecological environment. There is an urgent need to develop a detection method to achieve fast and synchronous detection of these pollutants without cross-interference. Here, a carbon dots-doped lanthanide-based fluorescent nanosensor (CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu) was synthesized, which can detect TC in the 380 nm channel by “antenna effect” and internal filtering effects (IFE), and identify Cu2+ in the 320 nm channel. The sensor was highly sensitive to TC within 0–4 μM with a detection limit as low as 3.64 nM, and Cu2+ could be detected within 0–40 μM with a detection limit of 38 nM. A portable dual-channel visual fluorescence sensor was obtained by loading the probes onto test paper and cotton swabs in food samples, which indicates the practicability of this sensing strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of animal husbandry has led to a large increase in related pollutants, and the related risks have gradually increased. Accurate and quantitative identification of these environmental pollutants is crucial [1,2]. Tetracycline and copper ion are important additives in animal feed and play a key role in disease prevention and growth regulation [3,4]. Tetracycline (TC), a broad-spectrum antibiotic, has been used as a warhead against various infectious diseases since its invention in the 1940s [5]. However, the continuous and uncontrolled application of TC has led to the emergence of multi-drug-resistant pathogens [6]. Moreover, TC is structurally stable and difficult to degrade, and it is continuously enriched through the food chain, posing a great danger to human health. Currently, the maximum residue levels for TC in milk stipulated by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) of the United States and the European Union are 0.676 μM and 0.225 μM, respectively. In addition, copper ion (Cu2+) is a standard metal ion, which ranks third among the essential trace metal elements in the human body [7], and plays a vital role in maintaining the everyday activities of living organisms [8]. Cu2+ is widely present in wine. Grapes contain endogenous Cu2+, which is sprayed with Bordeaux solution to inhibit molds during grape growth, and Cu2+ enters the wine in containers during winemaking and storage [9]. The residual amount of Cu2+ in wine is stipulated in the national wine standard of China, and the maximum residual amount of Cu2+ does not exceed 1 mg/L. The over-enrichment of Cu2+ as a heavy metal ion in the human body can cause digestive damage and neurological disorders, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases [10]. Therefore, developing novel multifunctional materials for efficient and on-site detection of TC and Cu2+ concentration is of great importance in the field of food safety [11].

Currently, the commonly used methods for the detection of TC include high-performance liquid chromatography [12], microbial inhibition [13], and enzyme immunoassay [14]. However, these analytical methods suffer from the shortcomings of complicated operation, high cost of instrumentation, and cumbersome sample pre-treatment. Ultraviolet spectrophotometry [15], atomic absorption spectrometry [16], and electrochemical methods [17] are commonly used for the detection of Cu2+, which also require the operator to have sufficient theoretical knowledge and skilled use of detection equipment. With the development of science and technology, analytical methods are also progressing, and more and more analytical methods are being applied to the detection of environmental pollutants. Compared with the above detection methods, fluorescence analysis has the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, and high visualization, which has been rapidly developed and widely studied in the field of analytical chemistry [18]. Among many fluorescence detection methods, ratio fluorescence detection has attracted much attention because of its strong anti-interference ability, independence from fluorescent probe concentration, and visualization [19], and was widely used for on-site monitoring of environmental pollutants. Previously, there have been many reports on the synthesis of fluorescent probes for single analyte detection of TC or Cu2+ [20,21]. However, the coexistence of multiple pollutants in actual environmental samples will have an unpredictable impact on the sensor during the detection process [22]. The development of sensors with strong anti-interference and high selectivity is important. Moreover, it would be very meaningful to design a multifunctional sensor to realize the cooperative real-time detection of multiple pollutants without cross-interference, but it is very challenging.

Lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) are typically organic-inorganic hybrid materials with intramolecular pores constructed from lanthanide metal-ion nodes by orderly splicing of organic ligands through ligand bonding [23]. With characteristic intrinsic luminescence properties covering a wide spectral range of luminescence from the ultraviolet (UV) to the near-infrared (NIR), Ln-MOFs have been widely used in optical applications such as sensing [24], illumination [25], anti-counterfeiting [26] and other optical fields for a wide range of applications. Especially in recent decades, Ln-MOFs have flourished in the sensing field due to their highly stable and tunable luminescent signals, high quantum yields, and longer lifetimes [27]. Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with significant fluorescence properties with sizes less than 10 nm [28], which have many advantages such as good water solubility, low toxicity, small size, and a comprehensive source of raw materials [29]. Due to their high photostability, CDs are excellent materials for use as fluorescent reference signals and have a wide range of applications in environmental detection [30], bioimaging [31], catalysis [32], and other fields. The composites of MOFs and CDs can be prepared by in situ synthesis method, in which the CDs are confined in the pores of MOFs, and more unique composites with more unique properties are expected to be obtained under the nano-restricted domain effect [33].

Based on the above research background, a nanocomposite CDs@Tb-MOFs was constructed for synchronous detection of TC and Cu2+ without cross-interference in the different channels. After coating a layer of amino-rich SiO2 shells on its surface using a functional modification method, a rare-earth europium was introduced to the surface of CDs@Tb-MOFs by covalent grafting of tetraethyl 1,1,2,2-ethanetetracarboxylate to obtain a nanocomposite fluorescent probe that can achieve dual-channel detection of TC and Cu2+ (Scheme 1 ). The results showed that TC with a β-diketone structure could sensitize Eu3+ through the antenna effect and emit red fluorescence under 380 nm UV excitation. In comparison, Cu2+ could quench the fluorescence of the nanoprobe under 320 nm UV excitation through the internal filter effect (IFE). The study also designed a functionalized test paper loaded with fluorescent probes for sensitive and rapid real-time detection of TC and Cu2+ with a portable UV lamp and a smartphone. In addition, a portable cotton swab was applied to simulate the qualitative analysis of TC and Cu2+ on the surface of several fruits, which fully demonstrated the potential practical application of this fluorescent nanoprobe. Finally, a logic gate circuit capable of rapid and intelligent semi-quantitative detection was also designed to provide a theoretical basis for the realization of on-site intelligent analysis of TC and Cu2+.
The reagents involved in this study, synthetic methods, experimental apparatus, and the construction of probe-based assays for TC and Cu2+ were listed in the supporting information.

The material was characterized morphologically using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images in Fig. 1 a and b indicated the successful preparation of CDs and Tb-MOF. The surface of CDs@Tb-MOFs (Fig. 1c) became rougher than that of Tb-MOFs, and granular materials can be observed in the material, which suggested that the composites of CDs and Tb-MOFs were successful. Fig. 1d showed the TEM photo of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2, and it was easy to see that the material was divided into two layers. The inner one was the CDs@Tb-MOFs composite material, and the outer one was the SiO2–NH2 shell formed by the hydrolyzed and deposited on the surface by van der Waals' forces of TEOS and APTES, which can effectively prevent the desorption of the CDs from the pores of the MOFs. Fig. 1e illustrated the final material after grafting the ligand and europium ions without any obvious change in the material's morphology. In addition, from the EDS energy spectrum results of Fig. 1f, it can be seen that in the final material, there were a small amount of Eu elements in addition to the elements of C, N, O, B, Tb, and Si, which proved that the Eu ions were successfully loaded into the material.

The XRD pattern (Fig. S1a) of Tb-MOFs showed no crystalline diffraction peaks because of the difficulty in synthesizing single crystals due to the use of 1,3-BDC and 5-BOP ligands [34]. The appearance of a broad diffraction peak at around 21° for CDs@Tb-MOFs compared to Tb-MOFs can be attributed to the carbon skeleton inside the particles formed by cross-linking or carbonization of the attached CDs during the formation process [35]. By coating SiO2 and modifying the amino group, the final 25° peak was significantly enhanced, indicating that APTES hydrolysis in the material suggested the formation of amorphous SiO2 [36], and the XRD curves of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu showed that subsequent grafting of ligands and Eu3+ coordination did not change the crystal configuration.

The material was further analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S1b). The absorption peak of 1051 cm−1 was caused by the vibration of the Si–O–Si skeleton [37], which proved that SiO2 was encapsulated on the surface of CDs@Tb-MOFs and the amino group was modified on the surface, and 1658 cm−1 was the absorption peak of -CO-NH-. The –NH2 absorption peak at 1605 cm−1 was red-shifted compared to that of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2 [38]. This is due to the acylation reaction of –NH2 with -COO- to generate amide, which proved that the ligand was grafted to the surface of the material by reacting with the amino group [39]. The absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 was the antisymmetric stretching vibration peak of nitrate after europium nitrate coordination [40], indicating that the Eu ions were successfully coordinated with the β-diketone structure in the ligand, and the composite material was successfully prepared.

XPS analysis was used to illustrate further the elemental composition and chemical bonding of the nanoprobe surfaces. The full XPS spectrum of the composite is shown in Fig. S2a, which included the characteristic peaks of the elements C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Si 2p, and Eu 3d. Since XPS can only probe the elemental composition 3–10 nm from the surface of a material, the characteristic peaks of elements Tb and B were not represented in the spectrum. It was demonstrated that SiO2 internally encapsulated the Tb-MOFs. Figs. S2b–f are the XPS high-resolution maps of individual elements of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu. The binding energy of 284.8 eV (calibration peak) can be attributed to the C–C coordination of the surface amorphous carbon. The binding energy at 285.97 eV corresponded to the C–O/C–N bond, and the binding energy at 288.64 eV belonged to the sp2 bond carbon (C=O) [41]. The N 1s peak can be deconvoluted into three fitted peaks of 407.25 eV, 400.6 eV, and 399.9 eV, respectively, which belonged to N–O, N–C, and N–H bonds [42,43]. The three peaks of O 1s at 530.6 eV, 531.45 eV, and 532.5 eV were derived from C=O, C–O, and Si–O bonds [44,45], respectively. The peak of Si 2p can be deconvoluted to a Si–O peak at 102.89 eV and a Si–C peak at 103.7 eV [46].In the high-resolution XPS spectra of the Eu 3d region, two prominent peaks were observed at 1164.35 eV and 1134.85 eV. Corresponding to the bilinear states of Eu3+ 3d5/2 and Eu2+ 3d3/2 [47], these peaks originated from the significant spin-orbit coupling within the Eu 3d nuclear energy level. In addition, two weaker peaks attributed to Eu2+ 3d5/2 and Eu3+ 3d3/2 were observed at 1155.5 eV and 1142.9 eV [48].

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the complex absorbed ultraviolet light and underwent π → π* transition, which meant that TC absorbed energy and transferred energy to the coordinated Eu ions through the antenna effect. The electron transited from the ground state S0 to the excited singlet state S1. Then, by intersystem hopping, it went from excited singlet S1 to excited triplet T1. And the energy transfer from the lowest excited triplet T1 to the vibration energy level of rare earth ions. The fluorescence peaks of the probe at 593, 618, 652 and 697 nm after the addition of TC corresponded to the 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 → 7F2, 5D0 → 7F3 and 5D0 → 7F4 transitions of Eu3+, respectively (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, a significant increase in its fluorescence lifetime occurred after adding TC (Fig. 2c), indicating that TC with a β-diketone structure was coordinated to Eu3+, and transferred the energy to Eu3+ through the “antenna effect”. This enhancement was also related to the decrease of coordination water. TC with a stronger coordination ability can replace the coordination water around Eu3+, suppressed the nonradiative energy loss caused by water molecules, and enhanced the fluorescence lifetime. The intensity ratio of the Eu3+ emission peaks changed with the concentration of TC (Fig. S3), also indicating that the coordination sphere of Eu3+ had changed [49].

Concurrently, the fluorescence peak at 450 nm showed a decreasing trend after the addition of TC. As shown in Fig. S4a, the UV–vis absorption peaks of TCs overlapped with the fluorescence excitation peaks of CDs, suggesting that the bursting mechanism between CDs and TCs could be either the internal filtration effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect. Afterward, by comparing the UV absorption spectra of CDs, TC, and the mixture of the two (Fig. S4b), the mixture phase did not show any new absorption peaks compared to the pure substance, demonstrating that CDs did not combine with TCs to produce new substances. Fluorescence quenching due to FRET can be excluded. Therefore, the fluorescence bursting of CDs by TC may be caused by the IFE, which had also been confirmed by fluorescence lifetimes in the relevant literature [50].

The monochromatic fluorescence quenching of rare earth ions mainly achieved the response of the probe to Cu2+. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cu2+ to Tb3+ was investigated by comparing the UV absorption spectra of Cu2+, probe, and their mixed solution. Fig. S4c showed no significant change in the UV absorption spectrum of the mixed solution after adding Cu2+, indicating that Cu2+ did not combine with the probe to generate new substances, which could exclude the decrease of fluorescence intensity caused by static quenching. By comparing the excitation spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra shown in Fig. S4d, it was found that there was a significant overlap between the two, so the quenching mechanism can be considered as the IFE or FRET effect. Fig. 2d displayed that there was no significant change in the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu before and after the addition of Cu2+, which can exclude the FRET effect and verify again that it was not a static quenching mechanism. In summary, the fluorescence response mechanism of the probe to Cu2+ was defined as IFE.

Firstly, the optimal excitation wavelength was explored. For the detection of TC, the blue fluorescence peak at 450 nm was used as the reference peak, and 593, 618, 652, and 697 nm were used as the response peaks. It can be seen from Fig. 2e that the position of the emission peak did not shift when the excitation wavelength was changed. Then, by comparing the fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs was lower when the excitation wavelength was more significant than 380 nm. When the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, the response of Eu reached a high level, and the fluorescence peak at 450 nm maintained a high fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the excitation wavelength at 380 nm was selected as the optimal condition to obtain higher sensitivity for TC detection. To investigate the effect of the pH of the solution on the detection effect, TC was assayed in Tris-HCl buffer at different pH. As can be seen from Fig. S5a, the best response of Eu was observed at pH = 9. When the pH was less than 9, the protonated TC cannot effectively transfer energy to Eu (III) ions, which lead to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of europium ions. When the pH was greater than 9, the fluorescence intensity of the probe decreased due to the formation of rare earth hydroxides by europium ions and hydroxides. The response time was also evaluated by measuring the fluorescence ratio at I618/I450. It can be seen from Fig. S5c that the response time of TC detection to was as short as 20 s. It can meet the needs of rapid on-site detection, indicating that the rare earth ions in the prepared nanosensor had a robust chelating effect on TC.

In order to obtain the best luminescent effect of the material, the fluorescence spectra of the material were scanned in different solvents. As can be seen from Fig. S5b, the fluorescence intensity was highest in ethanol, while the fluorescence completely disappeared in acetone solution. This may be due to the overlap between the acetone's ultraviolet absorption wavelength and the material's excitation wavelength (Fig. S6). The best excitation of the material was further screened, as shown in Fig. 2f, the highest intensity of the fluorescence peak at 545 nm was observed at 320 nm, which was chosen as the optimal excitation. In order to determine the response time, the fluorescence values of 545 nm at different times were recorded. As illustrated in Fig. S5d, the fluorescence intensity of 545 nm was stable after 4 min, so the response time was about 4 min.

In addition, the stability of the sensor probe before and after the addition of TC and Cu2+ under a 450 W Xe arc lamp irradiation for 800 s was also investigated. As shown in Figs. S7a and b, the relative fluorescence intensity of the probe decreased slightly after irradiation for 800 s, indicating that the probe had good fluorescence stability and photobleaching resistance. Within 30 days, the fluorescence intensity of the probe still maintained a good fluorescence intensity (Figs. S7c and d), indicating that the probe can be stored stably for a long time.

The detection performance of the probe for TC was studied in depth under the above-optimized conditions. With the increase of TC concentration, the fluorescence peak at 450 nm decreased slightly, and the characteristic fluorescence peak of europium at 618 nm increased significantly (Fig. 3 a). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I618/I450) showed an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of TC. As shown in Fig. 3b, the linear equation in the 0–1 μM range is Y1 = 0.9474X+0.1018, and the corresponding correlation coefficient is 0.994. The linear equation at 1–4 μM is Y2 = 0.3958X+0.6849, corresponding to a correlation coefficient 0.959. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.64 nM by the formula LOD = 3σ/D (σ is the standard deviation of multiple blank parallel determinations, and D is the slope of the standard curve), which was much lower than the TC residue in milk (0.676 μM and 0.225 μM) specified by the US and EU Food and Drug Administration (FDA). It was apparent from Table S3 that the probe in this study had lower detection limits and reasonable linear ranges than the published similar probes. The CIE coordinates (Table S4) were calculated from the emission spectra of different concentrations of TC, and the coordinate points were plotted in a CIE map (Fig. S8a). Comparing the fluorescence photos under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S8c), it can be seen that the color gradually changed from blue to purple-red. In addition, TC was also detected by individual CDs (Fig. S9). The detection limit was 96.1 nM, much higher than that of the prepared ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with increased Cu2+ concentration. The Cu2+ concentration was used as the x-axis, and the 545 nm fluorescence intensity change ratio to the y-axis was used as the fitting line. As shown in Fig. 3d, there was a good linear relationship between the analyte concentration and the change in fluorescence intensity. In the 0–10 μM concentration range, the corresponding linear equation was Y1 = 0.0565X-0.0050, and the correlation coefficient R2 1 = 0.967. In the 10–40 μM range, the linear equation was Y2 = 0.0168X + 0.3811, and the correlation coefficient R2 2 = 0.990. According to the linear equation, the detection limit of Cu2+ was calculated to be 0.038 μM, which was significantly lower than the residual standard of Cu2+ in wine (1 mg/L) specified in China's national wine standard. Compared with similar probes (Table S5), the probe prepared in this study had a lower detection limit. Table S6 is the CIE coordinates calculated by the emission spectra of different concentrations of Cu2+, and Fig. S8b is the CIE image based on these coordinates. From the fluorescence photos shown in Fig. S8d, it can also be seen that the green fluorescence of the probe gradually decreased with the increase of copper ion concentration, and the change rule was consistent with the rule in the CIE image.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability for the TC and Cu2+ monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the response of the probe to ions, common ions were introduced in the detection process. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the probe had no apparent response to the anions or cations, and there was no noticeable color change in the fluorescence photo (Fig. 4b). In addition, in order to investigate the anti-interference performance of the probe in the detection of TC, 4 μM of the interferents was added to the detection of TC at the same time. It can be seen from Fig. 4c and d that the probe's fluorescence intensity ratio and fluorescence color at 618 nm and 450 nm were almost unchanged, indicating that the probe had good anti-interference ability to external interfering ions. At the same time, the probe's response to other antibiotics was also detected. The results in Fig. 4e and f showed that the probe's response to other antibiotics was far less than that of TC, which further verified the specificity of the probe's response to TC. Similarly, when detecting Cu2+, the quenching effect of different anions and cations on the fluorescence of the probe was detected in order to exclude the influence of other ions on the probe. As shown in Fig. S10a, the addition of Cu2+ (40 μM) can significantly reduce the relative fluorescence intensity at 545 nm. Under the same experimental conditions, the addition of other ions (40 μM) did not cause significant changes in fluorescence, indicating that the probe had a better response to Cu2+. Similarly, the response of the probe was explored in the coexistence of TC and Cu2+. As shown in Fig. S10b, the response of Cu2+ and Cu2++TC was close to each other, proving that the coexistence of TC and Cu2+ had little effect on the detection effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of TC and Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection performance of CDs@Tb-MOFs@SiO2–NH2-Eu for TC and Cu2+ in real samples of tap water, lake water, honey, milk or wine was investigated by standard addition method. The calculated concentration of TC or Cu2+ to be tested was compared with the actual spiked concentration, and the recovery rate and standard deviation of the test sample were calculated. As shown in Table S7 and Table S8, the recoveries of TC and Cu2+ in actual samples were 92.0 %–110.0 % and 90.2 %–108.9 %, which were in good agreement with the previous results of our group by HPLC [42]. The relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) between the actual samples were within the allowable range of error, indicating that the fluorescent probe designed in this study had high reproducibility for detecting actual samples and can realize the quantitative detection of TC and Cu2+ in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalized test strips and portable cotton swabs for visualizing TC and Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, in order to achieve a more convenient and efficient detection of TC and Cu2+, a functional test paper for visual detection of TC and Cu2+ was designed. Under a portable UV lamp, the test paper's fluorescent color changes as the assay concentration increases. When TC was detected, the 365 nm ultraviolet light was turned on, and the test paper changed from blue to red with the increase in TC concentrations. For the detection of Cu2+, the color of the test paper became lighter with increasing Cu2+ concentrations when irradiated with a 302 nm UV lamp. In addition, the concentration of the analyte can be obtained by comparing it with the standard color card, or the RGB value can be identified by the mobile phone color recognizer, and the corresponding R/B or (G0-G)/G value can be calculated. The linear equations for the detection of TC and Cu2+ were fitted as Y = −0.7276*exp (-X/1.3458) + 0.7295 and Y = 0.0184X-0.0023, respectively, and X was calculated by substituting the Y values of the actual samples as the concentration of the substance to be measured.

Furthermore, in this study, a portable cotton swab was also developed to detect the possible presence of TC and Cu2+ on food surfaces. The fluorescent probe solution was encapsulated in a single-ended open cotton swab hollow tube. When used, the food surface was wiped first, and then the detection end was vertically downward, and the sealing end was broken. After the sealing end was broken, the solution in the hollow tube flowed downward due to gravity and atmospheric pressure mixed with the analyte in the cotton at the testing end. It was observed under a UV lamp to see if a color change occurred. Fig. 5b showed the fluorescence images of the detection end of cotton swabs with or without TC or Cu2+ under ultraviolet light irradiation; when there was no TC on the surface of the fruit, the cotton swab emitted blue fluorescence under the 365 nm UV lamp. When there was TC on the surface of the fruit, the fluorescence emitted by the cotton swab changed from blue to red. When there was no Cu2+ on the surface of the fruit, the cotton swab emitted green solid fluorescence under the 302 nm UV lamp. When there was Cu2+ on the surface of the fruit, the green fluorescence emitted by the cotton swab was weakened. The color of the detection end can be used to determine whether the food surface contained TC or Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of logical relationships in TC and Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: A logic gate circuit was further designed to intelligently detect TC and Cu2+ semi-quantitatively. As shown in Fig. 5c, the target to be measured was input 1, and “0″ was input when the target to be measured was TC, and “1″ was input when the target to be measured was Cu2+. In the output result, output 1 took the test object as the judgment criterion, and output 2 compared the excitation wavelength with the target detector channel as the judgment criterion. When the detector was TC and the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, the detector matched the excitation wavelength and output was (0,1), and when the excitation wavelength was 320 nm, the detector did not match the excitation wavelength and output was (0,0). Similarly, when the detector was Cu2+, output (1,1) indicated the excitation wavelength was 320 nm, and output (1,0) indicated the excitation wavelength was 380 nm. Use the output as input 3. When (0,0) or (1,0) was input, Output 3 showed (0,0,0) or (1,0,0), i.e., the detection channel was wrong and could not be detected. When (0,1) was entered, the TC concentration threshold was set, and output was (0,1,0) when the TC concentration was less than 1 μM and (0,1,1) when the TC concentration was greater than or equal to 1 μM. When input was (1,1), set the Cu2+ concentration threshold, and output was (1,1,0) when the Cu2+ concentration was less than 10 μM and (1,1,1) when the Cu2+ concentration was more significant than or equal to 10 μM.

In summary, a dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe with a core-shell structure was designed in this study. The probe realized the fast and synchronous detection of TC and Cu2+ under 380 nm and 320 nm channels, respectively, and had high sensitivity, high selectivity, and strong anti-interference performance. In order to improve the convenience of detection, the test paper was functionally modified, and the qualitative and quantitative visual detection of TC and Cu2+ could be carried out by an intelligent mobile phone under an ultraviolet lamp. In the rapid qualitative detection a portable cotton swab sensor was designed to realize the rapid qualitative detection of TC and Cu2+ after wiping on the food surface and observing under the ultraviolet lamp. In addition, logic gates and smart devices were designed according to the response phenomenon of the probe, and rapid semi-quantitative analysis of TC and Cu2+ was achieved, which provided the possibility for intelligent analysis of the target. The designed fluorescent probe had the characteristics of high visualization, short response time, portability of functional test strips and cotton swabs, and intelligence of logic detectors, which opened up new ideas for constructing other types of food pollutant detection platforms.

Fengzhi Yue: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaolei Zhao: Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiangzhen Chen: Validation, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Yongxin Li: Visualization, Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Huang: Validation, Resources, Methodology. Dan Zhao: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the foundation of the Science and Technology Innovation Team in Henan Universities (24IRTSTHN014), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (242300421247, 222300420047), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20162, 22205256, 51773052, U1904168), and the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2).

Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.
